section: Melting, Processing, and Properties of Disordered Fe-Al and Fe-Al-C Based Alloys

context_update.summary: This chunk motivates the development of disordered Fe-Al lightweight steels with 7 and 9 wt.% Al for transportation and civil engineering. A new cost-effective air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) technique is introduced, producing sound, porosity-free ingots comparable to vacuum induction melting (VIM). The air induction melting section explains that hydrogen gas porosity arises from the reaction of Al with moisture; 8 ppm H can generate 50 cm³ gas porosity per 100 cm³ of ingot. Ingots of Fe-7Al are sound and free from porosity/microcracks, while Fe-9Al shows porosity due to higher Al reactivity. The Fe-7Al alloy can be hot/cold worked into sheets/foils, with cold-rolled and annealed sheet exhibiting excellent room-temperature ductility. The role of carbon in Fe-7Al is mentioned without detailed results. Background information highlights the formation of Fe₃AlC₀.₅ precipitates in higher-Al alloys and their beneficial effects, as well as the mechanism by which lower Al improves ductility (reduced environmental embrittlement, absence of ordering). No figures/tables appear in this chunk. The following chunks are expected to detail processing, microstructural evolution, and tensile properties of Fe-7Al, Fe-9Al, and Fe-7Al-C alloys.
material_systems: Fe-9Al disordered solid solution with short-range ordering
processing_route: air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC)
hot and cold working to sheets and thin foils
mechanisms: hydrogen gas porosity from Al-moisture reaction
ductility enhancement by lowering Al due to reduced environmental embrittlement and absence of ordering

context_update.summary: This chunk details three melting routes for Fe-7Al and Fe-9Al alloys: air induction melting (AIM), vacuum induction melting (VIM), and the developed air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC). It explains that hydrogen gas porosity arises from Al reacting with moisture, and quantifies that 8 ppm H yields ~50 cm³ porosity per 100 cm³ ingot at a density of 6.7 g/cm³. AIM Fe-7Al ingots are sound, while Fe-9Al shows porosity. VIM at 10⁻⁴ torr, 1893 K pouring, produces pore‑ and crack‑free ingots. The AIMFC process uses a CaO–MgO–Al₂O₃–CaF₂ flux cover, 30‑kg heats held at 1900 K for 2 min, and yields sound Fe‑7Al ingots; Fe‑9Al AIMFC ingots exhibit microcracks due to higher hydrogen (0.0052 wt.%). Table I provides full chemical compositions for all process–alloy combinations, highlighting low sulfur (e.g., 0.0016 wt.% for Fe‑7Al) and excellent Al recovery (95–98%) in AIMFC. Desulfurization via CaO, crucible lining issues with MgO reduction, and the strong dependence of hydrogen pick‑up on Al content are discussed. Figures 1–4 provide process schematic, free‑energy data, cold‑rolled product photos, and hot‑rolled microstructures. Subsequent chunks should connect these ingot characteristics to further processing and mechanical properties, and examine the effect of carbon on Fe‑7Al.
material_systems: Fe-9Al disordered solid solution with short-range ordering
processing_route: VIM (vacuum induction melting, 10⁻⁴ torr, pour at 1893 K, 80‑mm molds)
AIMFC (air induction melting with flux cover, 1900 K, 2 min hold, flux composition)
key_variables: Al recovery (95–98% for AIMFC and VIM)
flux composition (~40% CaO+MgO, neutral CaF₂, Al₂O₃)
microstructure: hot-rolled grain structures shown in Fig.4
microcracks in Fe-9Al AIMFC attributed to hydrogen embrittlement
mechanisms: hydrogen gas porosity from Al–H₂O reaction
MgO crucible reduction by Al at ~1873 K (Fig.2)
flux cover barrier against moisture during AIMFC
figures_or_tables: Fig.2 (standard free energy of MgO and Al₂O₃)
Fig.4 (optical micrographs of hot-rolled Fe-Al alloys)
Table I (chemical composition of Fe-7Al and Fe-9Al alloys by process)

section: Air Induction Melting of Fe-Al Alloys with Flux Cover
previous_context_summary:
Summary: This chunk details three melting routes for Fe-7Al and Fe-9Al alloys: air induction melting (AIM), vacuum induction melting (VIM), and the developed air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC). It explains that hydrogen gas porosity arises from Al reacting with moisture, and quantifies that 8 ppm H yields ~50 cm³ porosity per 100 cm³ ingot at a density of 6.7 g/cm³. AIM Fe-7Al ingots are sound, while Fe-9Al shows porosity. VIM at 10⁻⁴ torr, 1893 K pouring, produces pore‑ and crack‑free ingots. The AIMFC process uses a CaO–MgO–Al₂O₃–CaF₂ flux cover, 30‑kg heats held at 1900 K for 2 min, and yields sound Fe‑7Al ingots; Fe‑9Al AIMFC ingots exhibit microcracks due to higher hydrogen (0.0052 wt.%). Table I provides full chemical compositions for all process–alloy combinations, highlighting low sulfur (e.g., 0.0016 wt.% for Fe‑7Al) and excellent Al recovery (95–98%) in AIMFC. Desulfurization via CaO, crucible lining issues with MgO reduction, and the strong dependence of hydrogen pick‑up on Al content are discussed. Figures 1–4 provide process schematic, free‑energy data, cold‑rolled product photos, and hot‑rolled microstructures. Subsequent chunks should connect these ingot characteristics to further processing and mechanical properties, and examine the effect of carbon on Fe‑7Al.

context_update.summary: This chunk reports 40% room-temperature ductility for a cold-rolled and annealed Fe-Al disordered alloy, and details the effect of carbon on Fe-7Al. Five Fe-7Al-C alloys (0.02, 0.15, 0.35, 0.5, 1.0 wt.% C) were prepared by AIMFC in 40-kg melts, hot forged, and hot rolled to 12 mm. The 0.02C alloy is single-phase, while higher C alloys contain Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates (XRD-confirmed, Fig. 6), with volume fraction increasing with carbon. Tensile properties (Table V) show strength improves from 396 to 610 MPa, but elongation drops from 18% to 3%. Hardness increases, indicating precipitation hardening by Fe3AlC0.5 as the dominant mechanism. Since Fe-7Al is not susceptible to environmental embrittlement, ductility loss is solely attributed to the rising precipitate volume fraction. The conclusions reaffirm that AIMFC yields sound Fe-7Al ingots with properties comparable to VIM, while Fe-9Al requires VIM to avoid microcracks, and carbon enhances strength but reduces ductility. Future chunks should note Tables IV/V and Figures 5/6 for detailed data.
sample_aliases: cold-rolled and annealed Fe-Al alloy (unspecified composition)
microstructure: Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates in higher C alloys
precipitate volume fraction increases with carbon
mechanisms: ductility decrease due to increasing Fe3AlC0.5 volume fraction, not environmental embrittlement
unresolved_terms: specific alloy composition exhibiting 40% ductility not explicitly stated
